facts_disclosed: Dr. Krause retired, switched to Frau Weber (CBT-focused)

turns.content: so i've been thinking about how to evaluate whether a new therapist is actually a good fit or whether i'm just settling because the alternative is a 9 month waiting list

That's a really important distinction to make. A few things to consider when evaluating fit:

1. **Do you feel heard?** Not just "they listen" but do they seem to understand the specific nuance of what you're dealing with?
2. **Do they challenge you?** A therapist who just validates everything isn't doing much. You want someone who pushes back thoughtfully.
3. **Therapeutic approach fit.** Different modalities work for different people — CBT, psychodynamic, humanistic, etc. Does their approach match how you process things?
4. **Practical logistics.** Schedule flexibility, insurance coverage, communication style.

What's the situation — are you switching therapists or starting fresh?

switching. my previous therapist, dr. krause, retired last month. she'd been working with me for years so she had the full context on everything. now i'm sitting across from someone new and having to decide how much backstory to unpack

Losing a long-term therapist is rough — it's not just a logistical change, it's losing someone who had years of context about you. That relationship probably took a long time to build, and now you're starting over with someone who's essentially reading the first page of a very long book.

A few things that might help the transition:

- **You don't owe the full history upfront.** Let context emerge as it's relevant. A good therapist will piece things together over time.
- **It's okay to grieve this.** Dr. Krause retiring is a real loss, even if she's not gone from the world. The therapeutic relationship was meaningful.
- **Did she give you a referral?** Retiring therapists often recommend specific colleagues for specific clients.

Who did you end up with?

yeah she passed along a few names. i'm seeing frau weber now, had maybe four sessions. she's quite different — very cbt-oriented. structured. dr. krause was more of the "let's follow the thread" type, more exploratory. frau weber wants to identify the pattern, name it, build a strategy. which is... fine? but different

That's a significant style shift. Exploratory/psychodynamic therapy and CBT operate on fundamentally different philosophies.

Exploratory work goes deep — it's about understanding *why* you are the way you are, following emotional threads wherever they lead. CBT is more pragmatic — identify the distorted thought, challenge it, replace it with something more functional.

Neither is inherently better, but they feel very different as a client. Some things to notice after four sessions:

- **Does the structure feel grounding or constraining?** Structure can be helpful — or it can feel like she's rushing past things that need more space.
- **Is she flexible within the framework?** Good CBT therapists adapt when something doesn't fit neatly into a cognitive model.
- **Are the tools actually landing?** If you're using the strategies between sessions and they're helping, that matters more than style preference.

How are you feeling about her so far — cautiously open, or more skeptical?

somewhere in between honestly. the cbt stuff actually maps well onto my adhd — breaking things into concrete steps, tracking when i'm catastrophizing about deadlines versus when the deadline is actually a problem. that part is useful. dr. krause was better at the less structured stuff though. the emotional processing, sitting with ambiguity, that kind of thing

That's a really perceptive distinction. For ADHD specifically, CBT is actually one of the best-supported therapeutic approaches. The executive function struggles — task initiation, time blindness, catastrophizing about deadlines — respond really well to structured intervention:

- Separating "I feel behind" from "I am actually behind"
- Building external systems to compensate for unreliable internal ones
- Catching the ADHD-specific thought spirals early

So Frau Weber's style might actually be a better fit for that dimension of your life, even if it feels different.

The question is whether she can also hold space for the less structured work when you need it. Some CBT therapists are flexible enough to shift modes — some aren't. Four sessions is still pretty early to know.

yeah that's the thing i'm trying to figure out. the adhd stuff is only one piece. dr. krause was really skilled at holding complexity — she understood how different parts of my identity interact with each other in ways that aren't always textbook. and she didn't need me to explain it from scratch every time because she'd been there

That context — the accumulated understanding of how your different parts intersect — is what takes years to build and can't be shortcut. It's one of the biggest losses when a long-term therapist leaves.

With Frau Weber, you have two options:

1. **Test her range.** Bring something to a session that doesn't fit neatly into a CBT worksheet — something that sits at the intersection of identity and emotion. See if she can hold it, or if she defaults to "let's identify the cognitive distortion" when what you need is space.

2. **Tell her what you need explicitly.** "My previous therapist was good at navigating the intersection of different parts of my identity. That's something I need from therapy." Good therapists respond well to direct feedback about what the client needs.

Four sessions in, it's worth giving her the chance to show what she can do before deciding she can't. But also trust your gut — if by session 8-10 it still feels reductive, that's data.

8-10 sessions as a benchmark makes sense. i think part of my resistance is honestly just grief. dr. krause was there through some of the most important years of my life. she saw me become who i am. losing that witness is... it's a lot

Yeah, that's grief. The word "witness" is exactly right — she wasn't just treating symptoms, she was present for your transformation. That kind of therapeutic relationship is rare and real, and her retiring doesn't erase what that relationship meant.

It might be worth telling Frau Weber about this directly — not the full history, but the grief of losing a therapist who knew you deeply. How she responds to that will tell you a lot:

- Does she honor it and give it space?
- Does she try to immediately problem-solve it?
- Does she show curiosity about what made that relationship work?

Her response to your grief about Dr. Krause is actually a good test of whether she can handle the emotional complexity you need.

i hadn't thought of it as a test but you're right. if she pulls out a thought record for "grieving your old therapist" i'll know something. and if she just sits with it and asks good questions that's also data

Exactly. It's a natural, organic test — you're not manufacturing a scenario, you're bringing a real feeling. Her response is genuine clinical behavior, not performance.

And honestly? Even if she does lean on a CBT tool initially, what matters is whether she can *also* be present. Some CBT therapists use the structure as a scaffolding but can step outside it when the moment calls for it. Others can't. You'll know within the first five minutes of raising it.

yeah. and the practical reality is that finding a therapist in berlin with a kassensitz who's actually taking patients is absurd. i went through the whole terminservicestelle thing, called probably 20 practices. frau weber was the only one who could see me within two months. so there's also a sunk cost calculation happening that i need to be honest about

The Berlin therapy access situation is brutal, and you're right to name that pressure explicitly. The sunk cost isn't just the time finding her — it's the knowledge that if you leave, you're back in the 9-month queue.

But here's the reframe: staying with a therapist who's "fine but not great" when the alternative is no therapist at all is still a rational choice. Frau Weber doesn't have to be Dr. Krause. She has to be good enough that you're better off with her than without her.

The bar isn't "is she as good as what I had?" The bar is "is she helping?" Those are very different questions. And you can always keep your eyes open for a better fit while continuing with her — you don't have to quit before you find someone new.

that reframe actually helps. i keep comparing her to dr. krause which isn't fair to either of us. she's a different person with a different approach and maybe that's not worse, just different. the cbt stuff is genuinely helping with the day-to-day adhd chaos, and maybe the deeper work can come later once we've built more rapport

That's a really healthy way to look at it. Rapport is the foundation — even Dr. Krause didn't start as Dr. Krause. It took time for her to become the therapist who understood your full complexity. Give Frau Weber the same runway.

And actually, starting with the structured CBT work might be a good on-ramp. It's lower vulnerability, gives her concrete things to help with, and lets you build trust gradually. The deeper work can follow once you know she's earned it.
genau. okay. i have a session with her thursday. i think i'll bring up the grief about dr. krause leaving and see how she handles it. concrete experiment, clear signal. very cbt of me actually

Ha — you're already absorbing the methodology. "Form a hypothesis, test it, observe the results." Frau Weber would be proud.

Good luck Thursday. And give yourself permission for it to feel weird — talking about missing your old therapist with your new therapist is inherently awkward. But it's also exactly the kind of thing therapy is for.

it is inherently weird. like onboarding your replacement and then asking them to help you process feelings about the person they replaced. meta-therapy. also i realize i should probably just be direct with her about what i need. i keep waiting for her to intuit it and that's not fair — she's not dr. krause, she doesn't have five years of context

That's a really mature insight. Expecting her to intuit what Dr. Krause learned over years is setting her up to fail. And being direct about your needs is actually a skill that makes therapy work better regardless of the therapist.

You could literally say: "I need someone who can handle complexity, not just symptoms. My previous therapist was good at that and I need to know you can go there too." That's not a demand, it's useful clinical information. Any decent therapist would appreciate hearing it rather than having to guess.

yeah. i think i default to testing people instead of telling them what i need and then being disappointed when they fail the test they didn't know they were taking. which is... probably something i should bring up in therapy lol

That's a genuinely excellent therapy topic, and the self-awareness to catch yourself doing it in real time is exactly the kind of thing that makes therapy productive. Bring that to Thursday too — "I notice I've been testing you instead of telling you what I need." 

That's vulnerable, direct, and gives Frau Weber something real to work with. If she handles that well, you've got your answer about fit.

this is turning into a therapy prep session which is kind of hilarious. alright, i've got my agenda: bring up the grief, be direct about needs, confess the testing thing. this was actually really helpful to talk through. sometimes you need to rubber-duck your feelings the same way you rubber-duck your code. danke

Rubber-ducking your feelings is a perfectly valid debugging technique. You've got a solid agenda — and honestly, going into therapy with clarity about what you want to discuss is one of the best things you can do. Go into Thursday with an open mind and trust your instincts. Bitte, and good luck with Frau Weber.
